message.content:
Repo: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay (branch harden/pass-2; pass 1 already merged 22 fixes). Venv (run PoCs): /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python.
Dataset-agnostic ML/methodology engine being hardened for PRODUCTION. Hard constraints (NOT bugs): local-first/data-sovereign (loopback only), no metered API (subscription OAuth), engine imports no adapter/dataset (ADR-0002), optional heavy backends lazy-imported (ADR-0006). Read real code; back findings with a concrete repro/PoC. Report ONLY genuine production-readiness defects, not style. Pass-1 findings #101-#122 are already FIXED — do not re-report them; DO report anything they missed or any regression they introduced.

Independent VERIFIER #1 of 2. A pass-2 assessor reported:
TITLE: Checkpointer bootstrap holds the process-wide _init_lock across a blocking, unbounded cross-process pg_advisory_lock + setup() — head-of-line blocking that can stall ALL checkpointer initialization (even for unrelated databases) indefinitely
DIMENSION: concurrency & load
SEVERITY: MEDIUM
FILE: src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py 195-211
DESCRIPTION: get_checkpointer acquires the single process-global _init_lock (line 195) and, while holding it, performs blocking I/O: pool.connection() (line 197), SELECT pg_advisory_lock(...) (line 198), and PostgresSaver(...).setup() DDL (line 203). pg_advisory_lock is a SESSION-level lock that blocks INDEFINITELY if another process already holds the same key (_MIGRATION_LOCK_KEY is a single fixed constant for all schemas). Because _init_lock is a single process-wide mutex (not keyed by conn_str), one thread blocked inside this section — e.g. waiting on the advisory lock held by a stuck or crashed peer process — blocks EVERY other thread's get_checkpointer call, including initialization for entirely UNRELATED databases / conn_strs that have nothing to do with the contended lock. Under concurrent run_study invocations that each build a checkpointer, this serializes all bootstrap and can wedge the whole process on a single peer's stuck advisory lock. PoC confirmed: a thread mirroring the exact (with _init_lock: ... advisory-lock-wait) structure for db_A blocks a second thread initializing an unrelated db_B for the full duration of db_A's advisory-lock wait (db_B blocked ~0.62s on a wait that, in production, has no upper bound).
SUGGESTED FIX: Do not hold a single global mutex across blocking cross-process I/O. Either (a) replace _init_lock with a per-conn_str in-process lock (dict of locks keyed by conn_str, guarded by a tiny meta-lock) so different databases bootstrap concurrently and only same-conn_str threads serialize; and/or (b) acquire pg_advisory_lock with a bounded timeout (pg_advisory_lock with a statement_timeout, or use pg_try_advisory_lock with retry/backoff) so a stuck peer cannot hang init forever; and only hold the in-process lock around the in-process check-and-set of _INITIALIZED_CONN_STRS, not around the DDL/advisory-lock wait.
Independently determine if this is a TRUE production-readiness problem. Read the code; PoC where applicable. is_real=false if false positive / already handled / documented-justified scope. Recalibrate severity. Give evidence.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
1	"""Durable run-state checkpointer — local, data-sovereign (ADR-0003).
2	
3	The orchestration graph persists its full state (current phase, gate decisions, locked
4	hypotheses, verdicts) through a checkpointer so a run can interrupt at a human-approval gate
5	and resume later. That state holds the entire analysis, so the store is **on-box only**
6	(ADR-0003); a SaaS checkpointer is disqualified.
7	
8	This module is the LangGraph checkpointer factory, ported faithfully from the prior
9	platform's hardened implementation and generalized (dataset-agnostic ``ASSAY_*`` env, no
10	config-file coupling — see ADR-0006). The hardened behaviors are deliberately preserved
11	because each prevents a real production failure:
12	
13	- a **self-healing ConnectionPool** (not a raw connection): when Postgres goes away — node
14	  sleep, restart, idle timeout, a network blip — a cached raw connection is permanently
15	  broken and every later checkpoint op raises, i.e. durability is lost exactly during the
16	  interrupt/resume the checkpointer exists to support. The pool runs a liveness check on
17	  checkout and replaces dead connections;
18	- a **session advisory lock** serializing first-time schema setup across *processes*, run on
19	  the *same* connection as the DDL (a pool would otherwise check out a different connection,
20	  leaving the lock ineffective);
21	- a **once-per-process-per-conn guard** (``setup()`` is idempotent but re-locking + replaying
22	  the migration query on every call is wasteful), with an in-process lock making the
23	  check-and-set atomic so concurrent threads don't both run setup;
24	- **credential redaction**: a backend exception (psycopg/psycopg_pool) can embed the full
25	  ``user:password`` URI/DSN in its own message, so the conn string is scrubbed precisely and
26	  the exception text via URI-userinfo + DSN-password backstops; the redacted message is then
27	  raised *outside* the exception handler, so the credential-bearing original is retained as
28	  neither ``__cause__`` nor ``__context__`` (a logger walking ``__context__`` cannot
29	  re-expose it);
30	- a single shared **atexit** pool-cleanup handler (one per process, not one per call) so the
31	  pool's worker thread is joined before interpreter finalization.
32	
33	LangGraph/psycopg are optional (the ``persistence`` extra) and imported lazily; the
34	credential-redaction and connection-resolution logic is pure and unit-tested offline.
35	"""
36	
37	from __future__ import annotations
38	
39	import atexit
40	import os
41	import re
42	import threading
43	from typing import Any, cast
44	from urllib.parse import urlsplit
45	
46	from assay_engine._local import require_local_uri
47	
48	_DEFAULT_PG = "postgresql://localhost:5432/assay"
49	
50	# Arbitrary fixed key for the Postgres session advisory lock that serializes checkpoint
51	# schema migrations across concurrent initializers. The value is irrelevant beyond being a
52	# stable per-logical-schema constant; "ASSY" as an int.
53	_MIGRATION_LOCK_KEY = 0x4153_5359
54	
55	# Guards the process-local one-time-init bookkeeping. The PG advisory lock serializes DDL
56	# across PROCESSES; this lock makes the in-process check-and-set sequences (atexit
57	# registration, the initialized-conn set) atomic so two threads can't double-register or
58	# double-setup.
59	_init_lock = threading.Lock()
60	
61	# Pools opened by the factory, closed once at process exit via a single shared atexit handler
62	# (registering one handler per call would accumulate handlers across repeated factory calls).
63	_OPEN_POOLS: list[Any] = []
64	_atexit_registered = False
65	
66	# conn_strs whose schema bootstrap (advisory lock + setup() DDL) has already run in this
67	# process — bootstrap once per process per schema; the advisory lock still guards the
68	# first-time cross-process race.
69	_INITIALIZED_CONN_STRS: set[str] = set()
70	
71	# Strips ``://user:password@`` (URI userinfo) from any URI-like substring. '/' is allowed
72	# inside the userinfo so a password containing '/' is still redacted; only '@' (the userinfo
73	# terminator) and whitespace bound the match.
74	_CREDS_RE = re.compile(r"://[^@\s]+@")
75	# Strips a libpq keyword/value DSN password token (``password=...`` / ``pgpassword=...``),
76	# handling quoted values that may contain spaces.
77	_DSN_PW_RE = re.compile(r"(?i)\b(password|pgpassword)\s*=\s*('[^']*'|\"[^\"]*\"|\S+)")
78	
79	
80	def _sanitize_conn_str(conn_str: str) -> str:
81	    """Return ``scheme://host[:port]/path`` with any ``user:password`` userinfo stripped.
82	
83	    Robust to malformed userinfo: a password containing ``/``/``#`` makes ``urlsplit``'s
84	    ``.port`` raise ``ValueError`` on access, so the whole parse (including attribute access)
85	    is guarded and falls back to the generic userinfo-strip regex / a constant placeholder.
86	    """
87	    try:
88	        u = urlsplit(conn_str)
89	        host = u.hostname or ""
90	        port = u.port
91	        scheme = u.scheme
92	        path = u.path
93	    except ValueError:
94	        return _CREDS_RE.sub("://", conn_str)
95	    if not host:
96	        return _CREDS_RE.sub("://", conn_str) if scheme else "<store>"
97	    if ":" in host:  # IPv6 literal — urlsplit drops the brackets; restore them
98	        host = f"[{host}]"
99	    netloc = f"{host}:{port}" if port else host
100	    return f"{scheme}://{netloc}{path}" if scheme else "<store>"
101	
102	
103	def redact_creds(text: str, conn_str: str = "") -> str:
104	    """Remove credentials a backend exception may embed in its message text.
105	
106	    Layers, in order of precision:
107	
108	    1. exact replacement of the known ``conn_str`` (handles any password characters, incl.
109	       ``/`` and spaces) — this is the precise layer and covers the real connection string;
110	    2. a generic URI-userinfo strip (``://user:pass@`` → ``://``) as a backstop for any
111	       *other* URI in the text;
112	    3. a libpq DSN ``password=``/``pgpassword=`` strip for the keyword/value form.
113	
114	    Layers 2–3 are best-effort for *secondary* endpoints (e.g. a replica DSN) the caller did
115	    not pass as ``conn_str``; the primary connection string is always scrubbed precisely.
116	    """
117	    if conn_str:
118	        text = text.replace(conn_str, _sanitize_conn_str(conn_str))
119	    text = _CREDS_RE.sub("://", text)
120	    return _DSN_PW_RE.sub(r"\1=***", text)
121	
122	
123	def get_postgres_connection_string() -> str:
124	    """Resolve the Postgres connection string (``ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL`` env → loopback default).
125	
126	    The env path bypasses any external config validation, so loopback is enforced here at the
127	    point of use (ADR-0003 defense-in-depth): graph state must not leave the box.
128	    """
129	    url = os.environ.get("ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL") or _DEFAULT_PG
130	    return require_local_uri(url, what="checkpointer connection")
131	
132	
133	def _safe_close(pool: Any) -> None:
134	    """Close one pool, swallowing errors so a single failing ``close()`` does not abort
135	    cleanup of the remaining pools at interpreter exit (``close()`` calls a C ``finish()``
136	    that can raise)."""
137	    try:
138	        pool.close()
139	    except Exception:
140	        pass
141	
142	
143	def _close_all_pools() -> None:
144	    for p in _OPEN_POOLS:
145	        _safe_close(p)
146	
147	
148	def _register_pool_cleanup(pool: Any) -> None:
149	    global _atexit_registered
150	    with _init_lock:
151	        _OPEN_POOLS.append(pool)
152	        if not _atexit_registered:
153	            atexit.register(_close_all_pools)
154	            _atexit_registered = True
155	
156	
157	def get_checkpointer(use_memory: bool = False) -> Any:
158	    """Return a configured LangGraph checkpointer.
159	
160	    ``use_memory=True`` returns an in-memory saver (tests / no-Postgres). Otherwise a
161	    Postgres-backed saver over a self-healing pool, with advisory-locked one-time schema
162	    setup. Raises ``RuntimeError`` (with credentials redacted) if the backend is unreachable
163	    or the extra is not installed.
164	    """
165	    try:
166	        if use_memory:
167	            from langgraph.checkpoint.memory import MemorySaver
168	
169	            return MemorySaver()
170	        from langgraph.checkpoint.postgres import PostgresSaver
171	        from psycopg.rows import dict_row
172	        from psycopg_pool import ConnectionPool
173	    except ImportError as exc:  # pragma: no cover - exercised only without the extra
174	        raise RuntimeError(
175	            "Postgres checkpointer requires the 'persistence' extra "
176	            "(langgraph-checkpoint-postgres, psycopg[binary,pool]) — not installed"
177	        ) from exc
178	
179	    conn_str = get_postgres_connection_string()
180	    failure: str | None = None
181	    pool: Any = None
182	    try:
183	        pool = ConnectionPool(
184	            conn_str,
185	            min_size=1,
186	            max_size=8,
187	            kwargs={"autocommit": True, "prepare_threshold": 0, "row_factory": dict_row},
188	            check=ConnectionPool.check_connection,  # liveness check on checkout (self-healing)
189	            open=True,
190	        )
191	        _register_pool_cleanup(pool)
192	        # Bootstrap the schema once per process per conn_str. The advisory lock and the DDL
193	        # must run on the SAME connection (pg_advisory_lock is session-scoped); a pool-backed
194	        # setup() would lock one connection and DDL on another, leaving the lock ineffective.
195	        with _init_lock:
196	            if conn_str not in _INITIALIZED_CONN_STRS:
197	                with pool.connection() as conn:
198	                    conn.execute("SELECT pg_advisory_lock(%s)", (_MIGRATION_LOCK_KEY,))
199	                    try:
200	                        # row_factory=dict_row makes this connection dict-rowed at runtime
201	                        # (what PostgresSaver requires); the cast asserts that runtime truth
202	                        # the type system can't infer from the pool kwargs.
203	                        PostgresSaver(cast(Any, conn)).setup()  # DDL on the locked connection
204	                    finally:
205	                        # Best-effort unlock; the lock auto-releases when the connection
206	                        # returns to the pool, and raising here would mask a setup() error.
207	                        try:
208	                            conn.execute("SELECT pg_advisory_unlock(%s)", (_MIGRATION_LOCK_KEY,))
209	                        except Exception:
210	                            pass
211	                _INITIALIZED_CONN_STRS.add(conn_str)
212	        return PostgresSaver(cast(Any, pool))  # dict-rowed pool (row_factory=dict_row)
213	    except Exception as exc:
214	        # Build the redacted message here, but raise it OUTSIDE the handler (below) so the
215	        # original — credential-bearing — exception is retained as neither __cause__ NOR
216	        # __context__ (a logger walking __context__ would otherwise re-expose the password).
217	        failure = (
218	            f"Postgres checkpointer connection failed (is Postgres running on "
219	            f"{_sanitize_conn_str(conn_str)}?): {redact_creds(str(exc), conn_str)}. "
220	            "For tests without Postgres, use get_checkpointer(use_memory=True)."
221	        )
222	        # The pool may have opened before bootstrap failed — close it and drop it from the
223	        # cleanup registry so a failed init does not leak the connection/worker thread (#105).
224	        if pool is not None:
225	            _safe_close(pool)
226	            with _init_lock:
227	                if pool in _OPEN_POOLS:
228	                    _OPEN_POOLS.remove(pool)
229	    raise RuntimeError(failure)
230	
231	
232	def configured_checkpointer(use_memory: bool | None = None) -> Any:
233	    """Return the checkpointer the environment selects.
234	
235	    ``use_memory`` (or ``ASSAY_CHECKPOINT_BACKEND=memory``) chooses the in-memory saver;
236	    otherwise Postgres.
237	    """
238	    if use_memory is None:
239	        use_memory = os.environ.get("ASSAY_CHECKPOINT_BACKEND", "postgres").lower() == "memory"
240	    return get_checkpointer(use_memory=use_memory)
241

content.text:
The code matches the finding description exactly. Let me analyze the claims carefully.

The finding makes two related claims:
1. `_init_lock` is a single process-wide mutex held across blocking cross-process I/O (advisory lock wait + setup DDL).
2. This causes head-of-line blocking: a thread stuck on the advisory lock for db_A blocks initialization of unrelated db_B.

Let me verify the key technical claims about `pg_advisory_lock` blocking behavior and the lock scope.

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && git log --oneline -5 && grep -rn "_init_lock\|_MIGRATION_LOCK_KEY\|get_checkpointer" src/ tests/ 2>/dev/null | head -40
input.description: Check usage of init lock and checkpointer

content.text: Now let me write a PoC to confirm the head-of-line blocking claim. I'll mirror the exact structure: a thread that holds `_init_lock` while blocked on a simulated advisory-lock wait, and a second thread initializing a different conn_str.

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && cat > /tmp/poc_hol.py << 'EOF'
import threading, time

# Mirror the exact structure from checkpoint.py:195-211
_init_lock = threading.Lock()
_INITIALIZED = set()

def advisory_wait(duration):
    # simulates SELECT pg_advisory_lock(...) blocking on a stuck peer
    time.sleep(duration)

def get_checkpointer(conn_str, advisory_block):
    with _init_lock:                       # line 195 — single process-wide mutex
        if conn_str not in _INITIALIZED:
            # pool.connection() + pg_advisory_lock + setup() — all blocking I/O
            advisory_wait(advisory_block)  # line 198 blocks INDEFINITELY in prod
            _INITIALIZED.add(conn_str)

timings = {}
def run(name, conn_str, block):
    t0 = time.perf_counter()
    get_checkpointer(conn_str, block)
    timings[name] = time.perf_counter() - t0

# Thread A: db_A stuck waiting on advisory lock for 0.6s
tA = threading.Thread(target=run, args=("A", "postgresql://localhost/db_A", 0.6))
tA.start()
time.sleep(0.05)  # ensure A grabs _init_lock first
# Thread B: UNRELATED db_B, no advisory contention of its own (block=0.0)
tB = threading.Thread(target=run, args=("B", "postgresql://localhost/db_B", 0.0))
tB.start()
tA.join(); tB.join()

print(f"db_A init time: {timings['A']:.3f}s (its own advisory wait)")
print(f"db_B init time: {timings['B']:.3f}s (should be ~0 if independent)")
print("HEAD-OF-LINE BLOCKING CONFIRMED" if timings['B'] > 0.4
      else "no HOL blocking")
EOF
.venv/bin/python /tmp/poc_hol.py

content.content:
db_A init time: 0.750s (its own advisory wait)
db_B init time: 0.550s (should be ~0 if independent)
HEAD-OF-LINE BLOCKING CONFIRMED

content.text:
The PoC confirms the head-of-line blocking structurally. db_B (an unrelated database with zero advisory contention of its own) is blocked for ~0.55s purely because db_A holds the process-global `_init_lock` while waiting on its advisory lock.

Let me check how the engine uses this and whether multiple conn_strs ever occur.

content.text:
Now I understand the architecture. The checkpointer takes the conn_str from `ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL` (a single process-wide env var), defaulting to one loopback DB. Let me assess the realistic multi-conn_str scenario.

The key findings:

1. **Head-of-line blocking is structurally real** — PoC confirms it. A single global `_init_lock` held across blocking advisory-lock + DDL serializes ALL bootstrap.

2. **`pg_advisory_lock` does block indefinitely** — this is standard Postgres semantics. A session-level advisory lock held by a stuck/crashed-but-connection-alive peer will block any waiter with no timeout. This is a correct concern even for the SAME conn_str.

3. **However, the "unrelated databases" framing is weak in this codebase.** The conn_str comes from a single process-wide env var `ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL`. In practice, all `get_checkpointer` calls in one process resolve to the *same* conn_str. The "different db_B" scenario requires the env var to change between calls — not a normal pattern. So the cross-database HOL claim is largely theoretical here.

4. **But even within a SINGLE conn_str, the more important real risk remains:** holding `_init_lock` across an unbounded `pg_advisory_lock` wait. If a peer process crashes while holding the advisory lock but its Postgres session survives (e.g., TCP keepalive not yet expired, or the backend not yet reaped), the first-time bootstrap blocks forever, and every concurrent `get_checkpointer` in this process is stuck behind `_init_lock`. This is a genuine availability concern.

Let me verify the indefinite-wait claim is not mitigated by any statement_timeout or connection timeout config.

Confirmed: no `statement_timeout`, no `lock_timeout`, and `pg_advisory_lock` (blocking variant, not `pg_try_advisory_lock`) is used. So the advisory-lock wait is genuinely unbounded, and it's held under the process-global `_init_lock`.

Let me check the ConnectionPool timeout — whether `pool.connection()` itself could block under `_init_lock`, which is another part of the claim.

content.text:
I now have a complete picture. Let me finalize the assessment.

**Verdict: The finding is REAL but its severity is overstated as framed, and the strongest part is mislabeled.**

What is genuinely true and is a production-readiness defect:
- The process-global `_init_lock` is held across an **unbounded** blocking `pg_advisory_lock` wait + DDL (lines 195-211). PoC confirms HOL blocking structurally. No `lock_timeout`/`statement_timeout` exists; the blocking `pg_advisory_lock` variant is used, so a stuck peer holding the advisory lock makes first-time bootstrap hang forever, and `_init_lock` propagates that hang to every concurrent `get_checkpointer` in the process. This is a real availability/liveness defect.

What is overstated/weak:
- The "unrelated databases / different conn_strs" angle is largely theoretical in this codebase: the conn_str derives from a single process-wide env var `ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL`, so in normal operation all calls resolve to the *same* conn_str. The dramatic "stalls init for entirely unrelated databases" framing requires a non-standard pattern (mutating the env var mid-process). The real harm is same-conn_str concurrent bootstrap all wedging on one unbounded advisory wait.
- The advisory lock + DDL only runs on **first-time bootstrap per process per conn_str** (guarded by `_INITIALIZED_CONN_STRS`). After the first successful init, the `with _init_lock` block does no I/O (just a set membership check), so steady-state is unaffected. The window is bootstrap-only.

Net: a genuine but bootstrap-only, low-probability (requires a peer stuck *with a live PG session* holding the advisory lock) liveness defect. The suggested fix (bounded advisory acquisition via `lock_timeout`/`pg_try_advisory_lock` with retry, and not holding a global lock across cross-process I/O) is sound. MEDIUM is slightly high given the narrow bootstrap-only window and the single-conn_str reality; I calibrate to LOW-MEDIUM, settling on MEDIUM only if you weight "hangs forever with no timeout" heavily. Given the unbounded-hang-with-no-timeout aspect is real and there's no recovery path, I'll rate it MEDIUM but note the cross-database framing is inaccurate.

input.reasoning:
CONFIRMED REAL, with a correction to the framing. The code at checkpoint.py:195-211 holds the single process-global threading.Lock (_init_lock) across genuinely blocking, unbounded cross-process I/O: pool.connection() (197), SELECT pg_advisory_lock(%s) (198) using the BLOCKING advisory-lock variant (not pg_try_advisory_lock), and PostgresSaver(conn).setup() DDL (203). _MIGRATION_LOCK_KEY is a single fixed constant (0x41535359) for all schemas, and there is NO lock_timeout/statement_timeout anywhere in the persistence package (grep confirms none). So a peer process that crashed-but-kept-a-live-PG-session, or is simply stuck, while holding the advisory lock will make first-time bootstrap block indefinitely, and because _init_lock is one process-wide mutex (not keyed by conn_str), every concurrent get_checkpointer in the process is stuck behind it with no recovery path. That is a genuine liveness/availability defect for a PRODUCTION runner.

TWO CALIBRATIONS that reduce its impact vs. the assessor's framing:
(1) The 'stalls init for entirely UNRELATED databases / conn_strs' angle is largely theoretical in THIS codebase. conn_str is resolved from a single process-wide env var ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL (get_postgres_connection_string, line 129); in normal operation all get_checkpointer calls in one process resolve to the SAME conn_str. The cross-database HOL scenario requires mutating that env var mid-process, which is not a normal pattern. The real harm is same-conn_str concurrent bootstrap all wedging on one unbounded advisory wait — still a real bug, but the 'unrelated DBs' dramatization is inaccurate.
(2) The advisory-lock + DDL section is bootstrap-only: it is guarded by `if conn_str not in _INITIALIZED_CONN_STRS` (196), and after the first successful init the _init_lock block does only an in-memory set check with no I/O (212+ uses the pool directly). Steady-state operation is unaffected; the hang window is strictly first-time-per-process-per-conn bootstrap, and the triggering condition (a peer holding the session advisory lock with a still-live PG backend) is low-probability.

Net: a real, bootstrap-window, low-probability but unrecoverable (no timeout) liveness defect. The suggested fix is correct and appropriate: use pg_try_advisory_lock with retry/backoff or set lock_timeout/statement_timeout to bound the wait, and do not hold a global mutex across cross-process I/O (only hold an in-process lock around the check-and-set of _INITIALIZED_CONN_STRS). MEDIUM is defensible because 'blocks forever with no timeout and no recovery' is the dominant factor; I would not go higher given the narrow bootstrap-only window and the single-conn_str reality.
input.evidence:
File /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py.

The lock and its blocking body (195-211):
  with _init_lock:
      if conn_str not in _INITIALIZED_CONN_STRS:
          with pool.connection() as conn:                       # blocking checkout
              conn.execute("SELECT pg_advisory_lock(%s)", (_MIGRATION_LOCK_KEY,))  # UNBOUNDED blocking wait
              try:
                  PostgresSaver(cast(Any, conn)).setup()         # DDL under the global lock
              finally:
                  ... pg_advisory_unlock ...
          _INITIALIZED_CONN_STRS.add(conn_str)

_init_lock is a single global mutex: checkpoint.py:59 `_init_lock = threading.Lock()` (not keyed by conn_str). _MIGRATION_LOCK_KEY is one fixed constant: line 53 `_MIGRATION_LOCK_KEY = 0x4153_5359`. Blocking variant pg_advisory_lock (not pg_try_advisory_lock) at line 198. No timeout: `grep -rn 'statement_timeout\|lock_timeout\|timeout\|pg_try_advisory\|connect_timeout' src/assay_engine/persistence/` returns only an unrelated doc-comment hit — no lock/statement timeout is set, and the pool is created without a connection timeout (lines 183-190).

PoC (/tmp/poc_hol.py, run with /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python) mirroring the exact with _init_lock + advisory-wait structure:
  db_A init time: 0.750s (its own advisory wait)
  db_B init time: 0.550s (should be ~0 if independent)
  HEAD-OF-LINE BLOCKING CONFIRMED
Thread B (unrelated conn_str, zero advisory contention of its own) was blocked ~0.55s purely because thread A held the process-global _init_lock during its advisory wait — confirming structural HOL blocking; in production the advisory wait has no upper bound.

Scope correction evidence: conn_str comes from a single env var — get_postgres_connection_string() at line 129 reads os.environ.get('ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL'), so concurrent in-process calls normally share one conn_str. Bootstrap-only guard: line 196 `if conn_str not in _INITIALIZED_CONN_STRS` and line 212 returns PostgresSaver over the pool without re-entering the locked DDL section.

content.text:
I've completed the independent verification. Summary:

**The finding is REAL but with two important calibrations.**

The core defect is genuine: at `src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py:195-211`, the process-global `_init_lock` is held across the blocking, **unbounded** `pg_advisory_lock` wait + `setup()` DDL. The blocking variant of the advisory lock is used (not `pg_try_advisory_lock`), and there is no `lock_timeout`/`statement_timeout` anywhere in the persistence package. A peer holding the session advisory lock (stuck, or crashed but with a still-live PG backend) makes first-time bootstrap hang forever, and since `_init_lock` is a single global mutex, every concurrent `get_checkpointer` is stuck behind it with no recovery. My PoC confirms the head-of-line blocking structurally.

Two corrections to the assessor's framing (which I weighed but which don't make it a false positive):
1. The "stalls init for entirely UNRELATED databases" angle is largely theoretical here — conn_str comes from a single process-wide env var `ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL`, so concurrent calls normally resolve to the *same* conn_str. The real harm is same-conn_str concurrent bootstrap all wedging on one unbounded wait.
2. The locked DDL section is bootstrap-only (guarded by `_INITIALIZED_CONN_STRS`); steady-state is unaffected.

I rate it **MEDIUM** — driven by the "blocks forever, no timeout, no recovery" property — not higher, given the narrow bootstrap-only window and single-conn_str reality. The suggested fix (bounded acquisition via `lock_timeout`/`pg_try_advisory_lock` with retry, and not holding a global mutex across cross-process I/O) is sound.
